infobox.value: in sports and other similar areas involving competition is a [medal](/wiki/Medal "Medal") made of [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") awarded to the third-place finisher of contests or competitions such as the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), etc. The outright winner receives a [gold medal](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal") and the second place a [silver medal](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal"). More generally, bronze is traditionally the most common metal used for all types of high-quality medals, including artistic ones. The practice of awarding [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") third place [medals](/wiki/Medal "Medal") in the Olympic Games began at the [1904 Olympic Games](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri, before which only first and second places were awarded.
["How are bronze medals decided at the Olympics?"](https://olympics.com/en/featured-news/olympics-bronze-medals-decided-losing-semi-finalists-races-playoff). *Olympics*. Retrieved 18 July 2022.

Swanson, Ana (2016-08-16). ["Why bronze medalists are happier than silver medalists, and other things the Olympics teaches us about human emotions"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160926230728/https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/08/12/why-bronze-medalists-are-happier-than-silver-medalists-and-other-things-the-olympics-teaches-us-about-human-emotion/). *Washington Post*. Archived from [the original](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2016/08/12/why-bronze-medalists-are-happier-than-silver-medalists-and-other-things-the-olympics-teaches-us-about-human-emotion/) on 2016-09-26. Retrieved 2023-08-19.

Medvec, Victoria; Madey, Scott; Gilovich, Thomas (1995). ["When less is more: Counterfactual thinking and satisfaction among Olympic medalists"](https://psycnet.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037%2F0022-3514.69.4.603). *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology*. **69** (4): 603–610. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1037/0022-3514.69.4.603](https://doi.org/10.1037%2F0022-3514.69.4.603). From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medal awarded to the third-place finisher of a competition

"Bronze Medal" redirects here. For other uses, see [Bronze Medal (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bronze_Medal_(disambiguation) "Bronze Medal (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Bronze star (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bronze_star_(disambiguation) "Bronze star (disambiguation)"), [Bronze Award](/wiki/Bronze_Award_(disambiguation) "Bronze Award (disambiguation)"), or [Bronze Medallion](/wiki/Bronze_Medallion_(disambiguation) "Bronze Medallion (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/1980_Summer_Olympics_bronze_medal.JPG/250px-1980_Summer_Olympics_bronze_medal.JPG)](/wiki/File:1980_Summer_Olympics_bronze_medal.JPG)

A bronze medal from the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Pisanello%2C_medaglia_di_giovanni_paleologo%2C_I_esemplare_del_bargello.JPG/250px-Pisanello%2C_medaglia_di_giovanni_paleologo%2C_I_esemplare_del_bargello.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pisanello,_medaglia_di_giovanni_paleologo,_I_esemplare_del_bargello.JPG)

The bronze commemorative [Medal of John VIII Palaeologus](/wiki/Medal_of_John_VIII_Palaeologus "Medal of John VIII Palaeologus") by [Pisanello](/wiki/Pisanello "Pisanello") (1438), made in various metals, but usually in bronze

A **bronze medal** in sports and other similar areas involving competition is a [medal](/wiki/Medal "Medal") made of [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") awarded to the third-place finisher of contests or competitions such as the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), etc. The outright winner receives a [gold medal](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal") and the second place a [silver medal](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal"). More generally, bronze is traditionally the most common metal used for all types of high-quality medals, including artistic ones. The practice of awarding [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") third place [medals](/wiki/Medal "Medal") in the Olympic Games began at the [1904 Olympic Games](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri, before which only first and second places were awarded.

## Olympic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bronze_medal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Olympic Games")]

Main article: [Olympic medal](/wiki/Olympic_medal "Olympic medal")

[Minting](/wiki/Mint_(coin) "Mint (coin)") Olympic medals is the responsibility of the host city. From [1928](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics")–[1968](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics") the design was always the same: the obverse showed a generic design by [Florentine](/wiki/Florence "Florence") artist [Giuseppe Cassioli](/wiki/Giuseppe_Cassioli "Giuseppe Cassioli") with text giving the host city; the reverse showed another generic design of an Olympic champion. From [1972](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics")–[2000](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics"), Cassioli's design (or a slight reworking) remained on the obverse with a custom design by the host city on the reverse. Noting that Cassioli's design showed a Roman amphitheatre for what was originally a Greek game, a new obverse design was commissioned for the [Athens 2004](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") Games. [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") medals have been of more varied design.

In a few tournament sports, such as [boxing](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Boxing at the Summer Olympics"), [judo](/wiki/Judo_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Judo at the Summer Olympics"), [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Taekwondo at the Summer Olympics") and [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Wrestling at the Summer Olympics"), two bronze medals are awarded in each event – one for each eliminated semi-finalist or for the winners of the *[repechage](/wiki/Repechage "Repechage")* brackets.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Psychological study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bronze_medal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Psychological study")]

See also: [Silver medal § Rejection of silver medals](/wiki/Silver_medal#Rejection_of_silver_medals "Silver medal")

In 1995, a study was carried out by [social psychologists](/wiki/Social_psychology "Social psychology") Victoria Medvec, Scott Madey and [Thomas Gilovich](/wiki/Thomas_Gilovich "Thomas Gilovich") on the effects of [counterfactual thinking](/wiki/Counterfactual_thinking "Counterfactual thinking") on the Olympics. The study showed that athletes who won the bronze medal were significantly happier with their winning than those athletes who won the [silver medal](/wiki/Silver_medal "Silver medal"). The silver medalists were more frustrated because they had missed the [gold medal](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal"), while the bronze medalists were simply happy to have received any honors at all (instead of no medal for fourth place). This is more pronounced in [knockout competitions](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament"), where the bronze medals are achieved by winning a [third place playoff](/wiki/Third_place_playoff "Third place playoff"), whereas silver medals are awarded after a defeat in the final.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) This psychological phenomenon was parodied in the [Jerry Seinfeld](/wiki/Jerry_Seinfeld "Jerry Seinfeld") special *[I'm Telling You for the Last Time](/wiki/I%27m_Telling_You_for_the_Last_Time "I'm Telling You for the Last Time")*.
